@graph.name: BMW X5 xDrive35i Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW X5 xDrive35i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned xDrive35i engine typically costs £4,500–£7,500 supply only, with the F15 variant at the higher end. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,500 depending on the specialist. In the UK, an engine replacement for a BMW can range from £9,000 to £13,000. A used BMW X5 engine typically costs around £3,000–£5,000. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your X5.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X5 xDrive35i engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N55 is a proven and capable engine, but it has well-documented vulnerabilities. The E70 (2010–2013) suffers from HPFP failure, water pump failure, and oil leaks. The F15 (2014–2018) benefits from refinements to the HPFP and cooling system, making it the more reliable choice. The N55 is generally reliable when properly maintained — regular oil changes and proactive maintenance of the cooling system and fuel system are essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW X5 xDrive35i?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most common issues are oil leaks from the valve cover gasket and oil filter housing, water pump and thermostat failure, HPFP failure on early models, VANOS solenoid failure, and charge pipe (boost pipe) failure. Early E70 models (produced to 12/2010) also suffered from delayed throttle response — addressed by a DME software update.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an xDrive35i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the generation and vehicle condition. Against a well-kept F15 worth £15,000–£25,000, a £5,000–£7,000 recon is 20–40% of the car's value — often worth it. Against a tired E70 worth £6,000–£10,000, the same recon may approach our 70% BMW threshold — get a scrap valuation first. F15-engined cars command a premium due to improved reliability.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW X5 xDrive35i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: E70 (2010–2013): N55B30A. F15 (2014–2018): N55B30A. Check the VIN plate or vehicle documentation. If your car is registered after 2018, it's likely the G05 generation with the B58 engine (xDrive40i) — not the xDrive35i.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the N55 water pump, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The water pump must be replaced — it's an electric pump with a limited lifespan (approximately 50,000 miles). When replacing the water pump, replace the coolant thermostat at the same time, as this is also a weak link. A preventative replacement at 50,000 miles costs £500–£1,200. Ignoring it can lead to sudden overheating and head gasket failure, which is far more expensive.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: X5 xDrive35i — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.problem: Oil leaks (valve cover, oil filter housing)
rows.whenItMakesSense: Straightforward gasket replacements — prevent oil loss and belt damage
Proactive replacement at 50,000 miles — prevent overheating
Viable repair — catch it before it causes further issues
Upgrade to aluminium charge pipe for reliability
Replacement cost may be justified on high-value examples

buyingChecks.title: Buying This xDrive35i? Check These First
buyIf: * F15 (2014–2018) with the refined N55 (improved HPFP and cooling system)
* Post-2010 E70 with HPFP and water pump replaced
* Documented service history with regular oil changes (critical for chain and VANOS longevity)
* No oil leaks around the valve cover or oil filter housing
* Water pump replaced as preventative at 50k+ miles
* Delayed throttle response software update completed (E70 produced to 12/2010)
avoidIf: * E70 (2010–2013) with no HPFP or water pump history — assume they're overdue
* Oil leaks around the oil filter housing (belt damage risk)
* Overheating history or unexplained coolant loss — cooling system may be compromised
* No service history — the N55 is sensitive to oil quality
* VANOS system failure symptoms — loss of power, rough idle

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The X5 xDrive35i is BMW's petrol V6 (straight-six) flagship SUV — a 3.0-litre single-turbo N55 engine delivering 306 bhp and effortless torque across the E70 and F15 generations. The N55 is a proven and capable engine, but it has well-documented vulnerabilities: oil leaks (valve cover, oil filter housing), water pump failure, VANOS solenoid failure, and HPFP failure on early models. The F15 (2014–2018) benefits from refinements to the HPFP and cooling system, making it the more reliable choice. The G05 generation (2019+) replaced the xDrive35i with the xDrive40i (B58 engine) — a significant step forward in reliability and performance. With typical X5 values ranging from £10,000 (E70) to £30,000+ (F15), an engine replacement is a major financial commitment — but the N55 is generally reliable when properly maintained. A well-maintained F15 with documented history is the safest choice.

cta.label: → Compare xDrive35i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW X5 xDrive35i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for smooth idle; any rattling or rough running may indicate VANOS or timing issues — see the map above
* Service History — Including which generation (E70 or F15) and oil-change discipline
* Oil Leaks — Check around valve cover and oil filter housing for leaks
* Coolant Level — Check reservoir when cold; any low or loss may indicate water pump or cooling system issues
* HPFP Health — Check for long cranking times or hard starting (early E70 models)
* Water Pump — Check for recent replacement (electric water pumps fail around 50,000 miles)
* VANOS Health — Test acceleration; any loss of power or sluggish response may indicate VANOS solenoid wear
* Turbo Health — Test acceleration; any lag, whistling, or hesitation?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate HPFP, VANOS or sensor problems
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
